title: Your first loop
instruction:
Think about a library. You don't write down each book's title 10 times. You scan the shelf once, and the scanner repeats the same action until every book is recorded.

A loop does the same thing. Write the action once. Tell it how many times to repeat.

Run this. It prints five shelf numbers without you writing five Println lines.
successMessage: Five shelf numbers scanned. One loop. That is the whole point.

title: The three parts of a for loop
instruction:
A `for` loop has three parts separated by semicolons:

`for start; condition; step`

- `i := 1` — start at the first shelf
- `i <= 5` — keep scanning while there are shelves left
- `i++` — after each shelf, move to the next one

`i++` means add 1 to i. It is shorthand for `i = i + 1`.

Change the loop to scan shelves 1 through 10.
hint: Change i <= 5 to i <= 10. Leave the rest the same.

codeChecks.message: Change the condition to i <= 10 to scan all 10 shelves.

successMessage: Start shelf. End shelf. Step between them. Three parts, infinite possibilities.

title: Use the counter inside the loop
instruction:
`i` is just a variable. You can use it inside the loop body — multiply it, add to it, print it as part of an inventory entry.

You already changed this loop to scan 10 shelves. Now add a line inside the loop that prints: `"Shelf [i]: [count] books"`

Assume each shelf holds 15 books. So the output should look like:
```
Shelf 1: 15 books
Shelf 2: 30 books
...
Shelf 10: 150 books
```
hint: fmt.Printf("Shelf %d: %d books\n", i, 15*i)  — use the loop counter to calculate the running total.

codeChecks.message: Use fmt.Printf to format the output.
Multiply i by 15 to get the book count: 15*i
Replace the TODO comment with your Printf line.

successMessage: The counter is a real variable. Use it to calculate shelf totals across the whole library.

title: Counting down
instruction:
Loops do not have to count up. Change the step and direction and they count down.

`i--` means subtract 1 from i each time (opposite of `i++`).

You are returning books to shelves starting from the top. Change this loop to count down from shelf 5 to shelf 1.
hint: Start at 5, condition i >= 1, step i--

codeChecks.message: Use i-- to count down instead of up.

successMessage: Same structure, opposite direction. i-- is your reverse gear for restocking.

title: while-style loop
successMessage: 1 doubles to 2, 4, 8... and hits 128 which exceeds the limit. That is a while loop in Go.

title: Infinite loop with break
instruction:
`for { }` with no condition runs forever. That sounds terrifying, but it is actually useful — when you do not know in advance how many books to scan.

You control when it stops using `break`.

This loop processes book requests and breaks when it hits request #5. Add a line that prints `"All requests processed"` after the loop ends.
hint: Add fmt.Println("All requests processed") after the closing brace of the for loop — not inside it.

codeChecks.message: Add fmt.Println("All requests processed") after the loop.
Replace the TODO comment with your Println line.

successMessage: break jumps out of the loop immediately. The code after the loop keeps running normally.

title: continue — skip and keep going
instruction:
`continue` skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps straight to the next one. The loop keeps running — just not for that particular round.

This loop prints section numbers 1 to 10 but skips even-numbered sections (those have no overdue books). Change it to skip odd-numbered sections instead (only even sections have pending returns).
hint: Change the condition from i%2 == 0 to i%2 != 0 — skip odd sections, let even ones through.

codeChecks.message: Skip odd sections by checking i%2 != 0

successMessage: continue skips. break stops. Two different tools for controlling your library workflow.

title: Nested loops — a loop inside a loop
instruction:
You can put a loop inside another loop. The inner loop runs completely for every single iteration of the outer loop.

This prints a grid of aisle and shelf coordinates. Run it and see the pattern.

Then change both loops to go from 1 to 4 instead of 1 to 3 — you have 4 aisles and 4 shelves to inventory.
hint: Change both i <= 3 and j <= 3 to i <= 4 and j <= 4.

codeChecks.message: Change the outer loop to go up to 4.
Change the inner loop to go up to 4.

successMessage: 4 aisles x 4 shelves = 16 locations. Nested loops multiply.

title: Running total
instruction:
One of the most common loop patterns: start a variable at zero, add to it inside the loop, read the result after.

This tallies books on shelves 1 through 5. Extend it to count books on shelves 1 through 100 and print the total.
hint: Change i <= 5 to i <= 100. The rest stays the same.

codeChecks.message: Change the loop to go up to 100.

successMessage: 1+2+3+...+100 = 5050 books tallied. Carl Friedrich Gauss figured this out as a child. Now you can verify it in three seconds.

title: range — cleaner counting
instruction:
When you just want to count a fixed number of times, `range` is a cleaner option.

`for i := range 5` counts 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — it gives you 5 iterations starting from zero.

Run this. Then change it to print `"Section 1"` through `"Section 5"` by adding 1 to i in the Printf.
hint: fmt.Printf("Section %d\n", i+1)  — add 1 to i because range starts at 0.

codeChecks.message: Add 1 to i so the output starts at 1 instead of 0: fmt.Printf("Section %d\n", i+1)

successMessage: range 5 gives you 5 clean iterations. Starts at 0, which is the Go convention — you will see this everywhere.

title: Fix the infinite loop
instruction:
This program is supposed to number shelves until it reaches 10 — but it runs forever and never stops.

Run it. You will see it keeps going (click Stop to halt it). Then find the bug and fix it.
hint: Look at what happens to i inside the loop. Is it ever getting closer to the condition being false?

codeChecks.message: Change i-- to i++ so the counter moves toward the condition.

successMessage: i-- was counting down, so i <= 10 was always true. Flip to i++ and it counts toward the exit. Classic bug — now you know to look for it.

title: Build a library inventory
instruction:
No starter code.

Write a program that:
1. Counts down from 10 to 1, printing each shelf number
2. Skips shelf 5 (it's being renovated) using `continue`
3. Prints `"Inventory complete!"` after the loop ends
hint: for i := 10; i >= 1; i-- { if i == 5 { continue } fmt.Println(i) }  — then fmt.Println("Inventory complete!") after the loop.

codeChecks.message: Print "Inventory complete!" after the loop ends.

successMessage: Inventory built. You used a for loop, i--, continue, and a post-loop print. That is a full chapter in one program.
